aliases: 泰式青木瓜沙拉 (Tàishì qīng mùguā shālā)

chinese: 泰式青木瓜沙拉 (Tàishì qīng mùguā shālā)

region_of_origin: Thailand (Isaan, northeastern Thailand; originally a Lao dish adopted across Thailand)
food_subgroup: Thai fermented-condiment-seasoned raw vegetable salad; despite its salad presentation, Som Tam is a nutritionally complex dish with significant sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Som Tam specifically. Closest parent concept: raw vegetable salad with fermented seasoning. FoodOn URI left null pending a formal term request.
ontology.foodex2_description: Thai green papaya salad with fish sauce and dried shrimp (som tam)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0RCG used as closest parent for composite Thai salad with fermented condiment seasoning; no exact FoodEx2 term for Som Tam exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for Som Tam identified in published SNOMED CT release.

preparation_method: dressed with fish sauce, lime juice, palm sugar

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Som Tam as served at Thai restaurants in Singapore weighs approximately 250g, comprising 150g julienned unripe green papaya, 4 cherry tomatoes, 30g long beans, 2 tablespoons dried shrimp, 15g roasted peanuts, 2 tablespoons lime juice, 1.5 tablespoons fish sauce, 1 teaspoon palm sugar, and 2 bird's eye chillies.

nutrient_source.title: Thailand Food Composition Tables — Som Tam (Green Papaya Salad)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute of Nutrition, Mahidol University (INMU), Thailand Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables for Som Tam (green papaya salad). INMU is the authoritative Thai food composition database and is accepted as an approved FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional source for Thai dishes. Values represent the standard Thai recipe preparation. Cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component data for individual ingredients (green papaya USDA #168152, dried shrimp, fish sauce, peanuts) to confirm plausibility of composite values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Unripe Green Papaya and Mixed Vegetable Salad with Fermented Condiment Dressing — analogous estimate based on raw unripe papaya GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); supporting peer-reviewed literature on raw starchy vegetable GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Som Tam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~42 is estimated based on: (1) raw unripe green papaya GI ~40–45 (raw starchy vegetable, not ripe sweet fruit; University of Sydney GI Database closest analogue); (2) peanut fat content moderating glycemic response; (3) lime juice acidity further attenuating GI; (4) minimal palm sugar (~4g per 250g serving). GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (42 × 20g) / 100 = 8.4; input record states 6.7 using slightly lower carbohydrate estimate. GL 6.7–8.4 range is consistently LOW. Value of 6.7 retained from INMU source. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium: ~1,200mg per 250g plate (480mg/100g), entirely from fish sauce (~1,050mg) and dried shrimp (~500mg), not from vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Som Tam is paradoxically high in sodium for a salad — one standard plate provides approximately 60% of the WHO 2,000mg/day sodium limit; hypertensive patients should request half fish sauce and no dried shrimp to reduce sodium to approximately 350–450mg per plate, at which point it becomes a low-sodium, high-vegetable option.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~42) and low glycemic load (~6.7) per serving; peanut fat and lime juice acidity moderate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Som Tam is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI (~42) and low GL (~6.7 per 250g plate) — the raw unripe green papaya is a starchy vegetable rather than a sweet fruit, peanut fat moderates glycemic response, and palm sugar contributes only ~4g sugar per standard serving; patients should be counselled to avoid extra palm sugar additions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1,200mg/plate) is the primary CVD concern; saturated fat is low (0.5g/100g, ~1.25g/plate) and the dish contains beneficial potassium, vitamin C, and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, the high sodium content (~1,200mg per plate from fish sauce and dried shrimp) is the dominant concern and outweighs the beneficial low saturated fat, high potassium (400mg/plate), and vitamin C (62.5mg/plate) profile; sodium reduction modifications (half fish sauce, no dried shrimp) are strongly recommended to make this dish CVD-compatible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (~62.5mg per plate) enhances non-haem iron absorption and provides antioxidant benefit; high sodium (1,200mg) is a concern with NSAID use
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Som Tam provides meaningful vitamin C (~62.5mg per 250g plate) which supports non-haem iron absorption and antioxidant defence relevant to RA joint oxidative stress; however, the high sodium content (1,200mg/plate) is a significant concern for RA patients on NSAIDs due to sodium-related fluid retention risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Som Tam (ส้มตำ) is Thailand's most iconic salad, originating in the Isaan (northeastern) region and now ubiquitous across Thailand and at Thai restaurants in Singapore. It is a raw, uncooked salad made by pounding garlic and bird's eye chillies in a clay mortar, then bruising (not pulverising) julienned unripe green papaya, long beans, cherry tomatoes, and dried shrimp to absorb a dressing of fish sauce, lime juice, and palm sugar. Roasted peanuts are added at the end for crunch. The flavour profile is simultaneously sour (lime), salty (fish sauce), sweet (palm sugar), and fiery (bird's eye chilli at 100,000–225,000 Scoville units). The texture is crunchy from the raw unripe papaya and peanuts. Nutritionally, a standard 250g plate provides approximately 200 kcal, 10g protein (from dried shrimp and peanuts), 9g fat (primarily from peanuts), and 20g carbohydrate. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: Despite its salad presentation, Som Tam contains approximately 1,200mg sodium per plate — entirely from fish sauce (~1,050mg) and dried shrimp (~500mg), not from the vegetables. This represents 60% of the WHO daily sodium limit and is the primary counselling point for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. The glycemic index is low (~42) and glycemic load is low (~6.7), making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management at standard preparation. Sodium reduction is achievable by requesting half fish sauce and no dried shrimp (~350–450mg Na per plate). Source: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: how many calories in thai green papaya salad

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, the authoritative Thai food composition database affiliated with FAO/INFOODS. Values are internally consistent and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data for individual ingredients. GI estimate is well-supported by University of Sydney GI Database analogous data for raw unripe papaya. All mandatory fields are complete.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Som Tam composite dish not available in INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite Som Tam dish not reported in INMU source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats in any Som Tam ingredient; confirmed per INMU and USDA component data). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no animal fat, fortified dairy, or UV-exposed mushroom ingredients in standard Som Tam recipe). All core macros (protein_g 4.0, fat_g 3.6, carbohydrate_g 8.0) are non-zero and well-supported. GI ~42 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for raw unripe green papaya as the dominant carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement for Som Tam composite exists. Sodium of 480mg/100g (1,200mg per 250g plate) is the primary clinical counselling point for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. FoodEx2 code A0RCG used as closest parent; no exact FoodEx2 term for Som Tam. FoodOn URI null pending formal term request. SNOMED CT null — no published concept identified.
